text: Welcome. I am presenting to you the demonstration of robotic pancreatic duodenectomy. Robotic pancreatic duodenectomy represent latest iteration of minimally invasive oncological surgery.

Many published series have shown twenty to forty percent morbidity and one to five percent mortality rate. Advantages of robotic pancreatic duodenectomy, less intraoperative blood loss, shorter length of hospital stay, similar oncological outcome as compared to open. Advantages over laparoscopy, high-definition three-D optics and endo-wrist movement.

We present a video of a peri-ampliary tumor and describe our institution's surgical technique. This is a video of a fifty-eight-year-old male with history of jaundice and fever since five months. ERCP and stenting was done on November, two thousand sixteen.
PET-CT showed hypermetabolic ill-defined soft tissue lesion in the periamplary region.

The surgeon begins the procedure by entering the lesser sac through the gastrocolic ligament. The right gastroepiploic vessels and right gastric vascular pedicle are transected. The stomach is transected just above the level of the antrum. Dissection is carried proximally along the transverse colon to complete the Cattlebrach maneuver.

The surgeon clips and cuts the right gastric artery, exposing the gastroduodenal artery. The gastroduodenal artery is then identified, clipped, and cut, providing access to the portal vein located just above the neck of the pancreas.

The surgeon identifies the superior mesenteric vein inferior to the pancreas and performs retropancreatic dissection. The anterior surface of the portal vein is free of vessels, and minimal counter traction is applied using suction to facilitate the dissection and tunnel creation.

The surgeon performs complete corporalization, approaching from below upwards. Dissection is carried out along the ligament of Treitz, delivering the transected jejunum into the supracolic compartment.
The surgeon dissects the gallbladder from the liver, isolates and identifies the common hepatic duct, and performs lymphadenectomy of the hepatico-duodenal ligament.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the pancreas at the inferior border of its neck. An assistant lifts the pancreas using a laparoscopic instrument to facilitate the transection.

The surgeon carefully dissects the uncinate process from below, clipping and cutting the inferior pancreaticoduodenal vessels. Other branches and tributaries leading to the superior mesenteric vessels and portal vein are similarly separated, with care taken to avoid injury to these vessels.
The robotic third arm retracts the specimen laterally and upwards, providing traction for safe dissection. Minimal counter traction is applied on the vessel, facilitating careful dissection.

The robotic arm provides increased dexterity as the surgeon divides the common hepatic duct just below the confluence and above the cystic duct junction. An enterotomy is made anteriorly on the antimesenteric border of the jejunum, enhancing access and visibility for the anastomosis.
In the robotic pancreaticoduodenectomy, the surgeon performs a hepatico-jejunostomy followed by a pancreatic jejunostomy, using 3-0 PDS suture materials for the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses the robotic arm to suture the posterior and anterior layers continuously with two separate suture materials. The bowel limb is sutured to the capsule of the liver to reduce tension on the anastomosis, with visible endorhiz movements aiding the suturing process.

The surgeon ensures there is no tension and some redundancy of the jejunum between the anastomosis sites. An end-to-side pancreaticojejunostomy is performed using a duct-to-mucosa technique. The posterior capsule of the pancreas is anchored to the serosa of the jejunum. The patency of the duct is checked, and an enterotomy is made on the jejunum.
The surgeon performs a duct-to-mucosa anastomosis using three-zero PDS suture material, with an infant feeding tube placed as a stent within the duct.
The surgeon utilizes the three-dimensional optics of the robotic system to clearly visualize the duct, facilitating precise suturing during the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon performs the anterior layer of the pancreatic anastomosis, suturing the capsule of the pancreas to the seromuscular layer of the jejunum using the same suture material.
The completed anastomosis sites are shown, with careful attention to ensure there is no tension at these locations.
The surgeon performs a gastrogynostomy, completing the anastomosis, and places a nasogastric tube beyond the anastomotic site to facilitate early feeding.

Patient was discharged on post-operative day eight and post-operative analgesic requirement was less and there were no complications. Robotic pancreatic duodenectomy can be performed safely and effectively
With demonstrated non-inferior oncological outcomes, the potential exists that robotic surgery may be the future standard in the surgical management of pancreatic diseases. Thank you.
